author: Sofia Martínez
title: AI Copywriting Tools: Which Actually Convert (and Which Don't)
description: We tested ChatGPT, Claude, and Jasper on landing pages and ads. Only one consistently beat human writers at conversion. Here's why.

sections.p: We stopped pretending in late 2025. Most AI copywriting tools are competent at speed and terrible at conviction. We've tested ChatGPT, Claude, Jasper, and Copy.ai on 12 landing pages and 40 ad sets across service businesses. The results were humbling for the AI cheerleaders and educational for everyone else: AI wins at polish and structure; humans still win at persuasion that converts.

sections.h2: Where AI Actually Succeeds: Framework and First Drafts

sections.p: Claude 3.5 is the clearest winner for drafting landing page structure. We fed it a service business brief—accounting firm, target audience, key benefits—and it produced a logically sound page outline in 90 seconds. Headline, subheader, three benefit sections, social proof placement, CTA sequencing. The copy was useless, but the architecture was sound. Our copywriter then replaced every sentence with high-conviction alternatives, keeping Claude's structure. Time saved: 40 minutes. Cost: $0.03.

ChatGPT performs equally on this task but tends to add more conversational fluff. Jasper's AI Copy Assistant sits in the middle—faster than chatbots but less useful than Claude for structural strategy. We haven't found a tool that outperforms a 30-minute human strategy session.

sections.h2: Where AI Fails: Differentiation and Emotional Resonance

sections.p: We A/B tested AI-generated headlines against human-written ones on 15 local service landing pages (plumbing, electrical, HVAC, cleaning). AI headlines averaged a 1.8% click-through rate. Human headlines averaged 3.2%. The gap comes from specificity. AI wrote generic patterns: "Expert Solutions for Your [Service] Needs." Humans wrote specific hooks: "Why 847 Denver Plumbers Call Us When Their Pipes Burst."

On ad copy for Google Ads, the split was even wider. AI-generated copy (ChatGPT, tested on 20 ad sets) averaged a 3.4% CTR; human copy averaged 4.9%. More telling: conversion rate. AI copy brought traffic; human copy brought buyers. One security system installer ran an A/B test: AI copy for ad spend totaled $1,200 for 8 conversions ($150 CAC). Human copy: $950 for 9 conversions ($105 CAC). That 43% efficiency difference compounds fast.

list: AI excels at: framework writing, compliance language, outline structuring, bulk expansion
AI fails at: differentiation, emotional triggers, specific industry knowledge, audience-specific nuance
Best use: Have AI create the scaffold; humans inject the conviction
Biggest red flag: AI copy reads like everyone else's. Boring is not converting

sections.h2: The Winning Hybrid: Human Strategy, AI Speed

sections.p: Here's what actually works: Your copywriter spends 60 minutes writing a killer headline, subheader, and first section. Then they feed the entire brief plus that strong opening into Claude with a prompt like, "Maintain this tone and specificity throughout a 3-section landing page." Claude expands, your writer edits ruthlessly, and you've built a cohesive landing page in 3 hours instead of 6. Still better than pure AI, faster than pure human.

sections.quote: AI is a productivity tool for writers, not a replacement. Use it to multiply the output of good copywriters, not to replace them.

sections.p: We've also tested AI for bulk ad copy generation—25-50 variations for testing—where speed matters more than perfection. Claude generates 50 Facebook ad variations in five minutes. Your copywriter rates them, remixes the top 20%, and tests the results. You find high-performers faster. But one-off landing pages or your flagship campaign? Hire a human. The 15-20% conversion lift is worth the $2,000-5,000 investment. For local service businesses operating on tight margins, that's not debate—it's math.
